title: Coverage Analysis of Rydberg Atom Quantum Receiver Arrays: A Stochastic Geometry Approach
authors: Dongnan Xia, Cunhua Pan, Hong Ren, Dongsheng Sui, Qihao Peng, Jiangzhou Wang
abstract: Rydberg atomic quantum receivers (RAQRs) offer quantum-limited sensitivity and broadband tunability. It is not obvious whether this device-level advantage also improves network reliability, since in dense deployments, aggregate interference can push the atomic transducer out of its small-signal regime. This paper addresses the question by embedding the RAQR front end into a stochastic geometry (SG) coverage analysis. Starting with the atomic master equation and balanced coherent optical detection, we derive a third-order complex baseband model that retains both the linear gain and the leading cubic nonlinearity. A Bussgang decomposition converts the per-element nonlinear response into an equivalent linear gain plus a distance-dependent distortion noise. Using this equivalent model, we derive the post maximal-ratio combining (MRC) SINR and obtain tractable expressions for the conditional and spatially averaged coverage probabilities. The analytical results show that RAQRs outperform conventional receivers in sparse deployments. However, when the base station (BS) density becomes large, nonlinear distortion reduces this advantage and may make RAQRs perform worse. Simulation results validate the analytical expressions and confirm that the central design tradeoff is between linear gain and cubic nonlinearity.
summary: This paper analyzes the performance of Rydberg-based quantum receivers in wireless networks using stochastic geometry. It reveals a fundamental tradeoff where the advantage of low quantum noise is lost in dense networks due to nonlinear distortion from aggregate interference.
main_problem: Determining whether the high sensitivity of Rydberg Atom Quantum Receivers (RAQRs) translates to improved network-level reliability in dense wireless deployments, specifically considering the impact of nonlinear distortion caused by aggregate interference.
main_result: RAQRs outperform conventional receivers in sparse, noise-limited networks, but in dense networks, nonlinear distortion (Bussgang distortion) can cause RAQR performance to fall below that of conventional receivers.
method: The authors use a stochastic geometry approach with a Poisson Point Process, applying Bussgang decomposition to linearize the third-order nonlinear atomic response and deriving analytical expressions for coverage probability.
model_or_system: A four-level ladder-type Rydberg atomic system (|1> to |4>) interacting with RF fields, modeled via the Lindblad master equation and a third-order complex baseband model including linear gain and cubic nonlinearity.
key_observables: SINR (Signal-to-Interference-plus-Noise Ratio), coverage probability, and effective noise.
important_parameters: Base station density (lambda_b), antenna array size (M_r), nonlinearity ratio (|c3/c1|), and path-loss exponent (alpha).
main_assumptions: The input signal is assumed to be a circularly symmetric complex Gaussian process, and the network is modeled as a homogeneous Poisson Point Process.
figures_summary: Comparison of 133Cs and 85Rb performance; coverage probability vs. SINR threshold for various densities; impact of array scaling (M_r) and receiver correlation on coverage.
paper_structure: The paper moves from a microscopic atomic master equation to a macroscopic network-level coverage analysis, establishing a nonlinear baseband model, applying linearization techniques, and validating results via Monte Carlo simulations.
why_interesting: It bridges microscopic open quantum system dynamics (Lindblad equation) with macroscopic network theory, exploring how nonlinearities in quantum transducers affect large-scale system reliability.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
